title: 🎙️ Arts, Culture & Digital Storytelling
description: Today we explore how Cariboo and rural BC communities create, preserve, and share culture — from festivals and community radio to digital archives and open-source arts tools. We're looking for stories where creativity meets technology in small-town contexts, and where local artists and storytellers are finding new ways to reach audiences beyond geography. - Daily themed article selection for podcast generation

_podcast.theme_description: Today we explore how Cariboo and rural BC communities create, preserve, and share culture — from festivals and community radio to digital archives and open-source arts tools. We're looking for stories where creativity meets technology in small-town contexts, and where local artists and storytellers are finding new ways to reach audiences beyond geography.
_podcast.theme_scoring_prompt: Rate how well this article fits the theme 'Arts, Culture & Digital Storytelling' through a RURAL TECH AND INNOVATION lens. Strong fits include: digital storytelling platforms and tools, community media tech (podcasting, streaming, local publishing), creative tech economy in small towns, rural cultural sector innovation, open-source arts tools, Cariboo arts festivals using digital outreach, preservation of rural cultural heritage through technology, animation/film/media production in rural BC, local radio/podcast infrastructure, and how rural communities use technology for creative expression. Prefer articles that bridge culture AND technology in rural/Cariboo contexts. Score 0-100 where 100 is a perfect thematic match and 0 is completely unrelated.

items.title: [NPR Health News] Engaging with the arts can slow biological aging, study shows
items.content_html: Eat well and exercise. That's the classic advice for living a long, healthy life. A new study suggests another habit to add to the list: Let your creative juices flow.

items.title: [Open Culture] When the Nobel Prize Committee Rejected The Lord of the Rings: Tolkien “Has Not Measured Up to Storytelling of the Highest Quality” (1961)
items.content_html: When J.R.R. Tolkien’s Lord of the Rings books appeared in the mid-1950s, they were met with very mixed reviews, an unsurprising reception given that nothing like them had been written for adult readers since Edmund Spenser’s epic 16th century English poem The Faerie Queene, perhaps. items.title: [ScienceDaily] Lost 1,200-year-old manuscript contains the first English poem
items.content_html: A long-lost manuscript discovered in Rome has revealed one of the oldest surviving versions of the very first known poem written in English. Hidden for decades and once believed lost, the 1,200-year-old manuscript contains Caedmon’s Hymn — a nine-line Old English poem said to have been miraculously composed by a shy Northumbrian cowherd after a divine dream.

items.title: [Boing Boing] Digitizing vintage proportional typewriter fonts
items.content_html:
IBM's Selectric Composer was a special edition of the famed business-class typewriter that used proportional type instead of fixed-width characters, allowing the typist to create beautifully typeset documents that appeared printed, decades before computers made it easy. Now we have computers, though, we want the fancy typewriters back. — Read the rest

The post Digitizing vintage proportional typewriter fonts appeared first on Boing Boing.

items.title: [The Marginalian] The Enemy Outside and the Enemy Within: Audre Lorde’s Antidote to Despair
items.content_html: “There is no love of life without despair of life,” Albert Camus wrote between two world wars. There are many species of despair — the private despair of ill health and heartbreak, the public despair we call politics, the existential despair of bearing our transience and our utter insignificance to the life of the cosmos. In the autumn of 1978, Audre Lorde (February 18, 1934–November 17, 1992) faced several species at once as a grim diagnosis first interrupted, then fortified her work as one the most personal yet most politically consequential voices of the past century. “The shortest statement of… read article

items.title: [The Marginalian] On Children: Poignant Parenting Advice from Kahlil Gibran
items.content_html: "Your children are not your children. They are the sons and daughters of Life’s longing for itself... You may give them your love but not your thoughts, for they have their own thoughts. You may house their bodies but not their souls, for their souls dwell in the house of tomorrow..."
